channel.title: arXiv keyword digest
channel.description: Daily arXiv papers summarized with Codex

item.title: Reasoning as Pattern Matching: Shared Mechanisms in Human and LLM Everyday Reasoning
item.description:
Priority: 50/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 44, Paper 45, Empirical 62, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 76

Missing quality signals: Affiliations not supplied., Citation counts or h-index signals not supplied., No DOI, journal, or conference venue supplied., No code repository or public data mentioned., No participant sample size or model list in abstract., No baselines, ablations, statistical robustness, or effect sizes in abstract.

Score evidence: Evaluates human participants and 25 LLMs on common-sense reasoning about everyday situations.; Reports similar patterns of errors in people and models.; Identifies attention heads driving LLM responses and characterizes them as pattern-matching.; Claims those heads predict human reasoning errors caused by irrelevant prompt details.

TL;DR: The paper argues that everyday causal reasoning errors in both humans and LLMs may arise from pattern-matching mechanisms rather than abstract world models.

Problem: The paper studies whether failures in common-sense and everyday causal reasoning distinguish LLMs from humans, or whether similar error patterns appear in both.

Method: The authors evaluate human participants and 25 LLMs on common-sense reasoning tasks across everyday situations, then analyze attention heads that drive LLM responses and test whether those heads predict human reasoning errors caused by irrelevant prompt details.

Key contribution: The main novelty is linking LLM attention-head pattern-matching mechanisms to parallel error patterns in human causal reasoning, suggesting a shared behavioral account rather than treating LLM failures as uniquely non-human.

Main result: From the abstract, humans and 25 LLMs show similar reasoning-error patterns, and identified LLM attention heads reportedly predict seemingly inexplicable human errors induced by irrelevant prompt details.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide task details, participant counts, model list, effect sizes, baselines, ablations, statistical tests, code, data, or robustness checks, so the strength and generality of the evidence cannot be assessed from the supplied metadata alone.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query 'causal reasoning' because it directly investigates everyday causal/common-sense reasoning mechanisms and compares human and LLM reasoning failures.

Tags: causal reasoning, common-sense reasoning, LLMs, human cognition, attention heads, pattern matching

Figure note: A useful image would be a framework figure linking everyday reasoning prompts, human and LLM error-pattern comparison, attention-head identification, and prediction of human errors from prompt details.

Authors: Zach Studdiford, Gary Lupyan

Query: causal reasoning, causality

PDF

item.title: Beyond the Commitment Boundary: Probing Epiphenomenal Chain-of-Thought in Large Reasoning Models
item.description:
Priority: 53/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 32, Paper 42, Empirical 68, Reproducibility 38, Manuscript 78

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, citation count or h-index, DOI, venue, or peer-review status, code repository, public data or benchmark names, exact model families

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-11 in cs.LG, cs.AI, and cs.CL.; Abstract says causal importance is estimated via early exit.; Abstract reports experiments across diverse tasks and several model families.; Abstract claims CoT length reductions up to 55% on average with negligible performance impact.

TL;DR: The paper studies when chain-of-thought reasoning actually determines a model’s answer and proposes causal/probing methods to identify a sharp “commitment boundary” after which many reasoning steps appear epiphenomenal.

Problem: Chain-of-thought is widely used for inference-time scaling, but the abstract argues that the causal influence of individual reasoning steps on the final answer is poorly understood.

Method: The authors estimate each reasoning step’s causal importance using early exit, analyze answer formation across reasoning traces from several model families, train attention probes to decode answer-formation stages, and use the detected commitment boundary to stop CoT generation early.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is that reasoning traces often contain a sharp commitment boundary: a transition to a stable high-confidence answer that can occur in a single step well before the reasoning block ends, followed by steps that do not change final-answer probability.

Main result: From the abstract, attention probes decode answer-formation stages with high accuracy and generalize to unseen reasoning tasks; early exiting at the commitment boundary reduces CoT length by up to 55% on average with negligible performance impact.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide dataset names, exact model families, baseline comparisons, ablations, statistical uncertainty, code availability, or failure cases, so the strength and reproducibility of the empirical claims cannot be judged from metadata alone.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the configured query “causality” because it estimates the causal importance of individual CoT steps and studies causal influence within reasoning traces.

Tags: chain-of-thought, causal analysis, early exit, reasoning models, interpretability, attention probes, inference efficiency

Figure note: A useful figure would show the proposed pipeline: step-level causal-importance estimation, detection of the commitment boundary, attention-probe decoding of answer-formation stages, and early exit from the remaining CoT steps.

Authors: Daniel Scalena, Sara Candussio, Luca Bortolussi, Elisabetta Fersini, Malvina Nissim, Gabriele Sarti

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Is It You or Your Environment? A Bayesian Inference Framework for Genomically-Anchored Personalized Physiological Interpretation
item.description:
Priority: 38/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 34, Paper 42, Empirical 25, Reproducibility 22, Manuscript 70

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, citation count or h-index, DOI, journal, or conference venue, open-source code, public dataset or validation cohort, baseline comparisons

Score evidence: Published as arXiv v1 on 2026-06-11 in cs.AI, cs.HC, and q-bio categories.; Abstract proposes a Bayesian belief state using GWAS-derived effect sizes and genotype risk-allele counts.; Abstract explicitly discusses reverse causation, Mendelian randomization, ancestry-matched effect sizes, and attribution constraints.; No quantitative empirical result, dataset, baseline, code, or external validation is reported in the supplied abstract.

TL;DR: The paper proposes a Bayesian causal-inference-inspired framework that uses genomic profiles as personalized priors to reduce cold-start problems in physiological AI interpretation.

Problem: Personalized health AI systems need weeks of behavioral and physiological data before they can distinguish a person's constitutional baseline from environmentally driven deviations.

Method: The framework initializes an individual's physiological set point using GWAS-derived effect sizes and genotype risk-allele counts, then interprets incoming measurements as deviations from this genomic anchor while gradually decaying the genomic prior toward an empirical personal baseline as data accumulate.

Key contribution: The novel idea is to use genomics as an exogenous, pre-observation anchor for personalized physiological inference, explicitly separating fixed constitutional variation from environmental or state-driven changes and grading genetic priors by evidence strength.

Main result: The abstract does not report empirical validation, benchmark results, or quantitative performance; it provides conceptual examples such as the same HRV value leading to opposite interpretations depending on the genomic prior.

Limitation: Evidence appears primarily conceptual from the abstract. Missing are empirical evaluation, clinical validation, baselines against standard personalization methods, robustness checks across ancestries, implementation details, code, and deployment evidence.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the configured causal inference feed because it explicitly frames genomic profiles as exogenous anchors, discusses reverse causation, Mendelian randomization boundaries, and causal attribution in personalized physiological AI.

Tags: causal inference, Bayesian priors, personalized health AI, genomics, physiological modeling, cold start, Mendelian randomization

Figure note: A pipeline diagram showing genomic prior construction, incoming physiological measurement comparison, deviation attribution, and dynamic decay toward an empirical baseline would be useful; no direct image URL is available from the supplied metadata.

Authors: Aruna Dey, Suraj Biswas

Query: causal inference, causality

PDF

item.title: NetCause: Counterfactual Learning for Root Cause Analysis in Large-Scale Networks
item.description:
Priority: 57/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 46, Paper 55, Empirical 72, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 78

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations are not supplied in the metadata., Citation count and h-index signals are not supplied., No DOI, journal, or conference venue is supplied., No code repository or public dataset is mentioned., No ablation study is mentioned in the abstract., No statistical significance or robustness analysis is mentioned.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-11 in cs.NI and cs.LG.; Abstract states NetCause models incidents as graph-temporal processes and uses counterfactual simulation.; Training uses over 1,500 production-network incidents collected over six months.; Evaluation uses 31 expert-labeled incidents.

TL;DR: NetCause applies self-supervised graph-temporal learning and counterfactual simulation to rank root causes of customer-impacting incidents in large-scale cloud networks.

Problem: The paper studies root cause analysis for large-scale networks where faults can propagate across complex physical and logical dependencies, making static rules, correlation heuristics, and topology-local reasoning brittle in dynamic environments.

Method: It proposes NetCause, a self-supervised framework that models network incidents as graph-temporal processes and uses counterfactual simulation to score and rank candidate root-cause hypotheses, with outputs intended to connect to operator-defined mitigation and remediation actions.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element, based on the abstract, is combining learned graph-temporal incident modeling with counterfactual root-cause ranking for production-network RCA, rather than relying only on static rules, correlations, or local topology heuristics.

Main result: Trained on more than 1,500 incidents from six months of a leading cloud provider's production network and evaluated on 31 expert-labeled incidents, NetCause reports a 16.1% accuracy improvement over a rule-based heuristic baseline in the operationally relevant ranking regime.

Limitation: The abstract reports computationally intensive training and a small expert-labeled evaluation set of 31 incidents; it does not mention code release, public data, ablations, statistical significance, robustness checks, or comparisons beyond a rule-based heuristic baseline.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the keyword 'causality' because it uses counterfactual simulation to causally attribute customer impact to root causes in network incidents.

Tags: causality, counterfactual-learning, root-cause-analysis, network-operations, graph-temporal-modeling, self-supervised-learning, cloud-networks

Figure note: A useful image would show the NetCause pipeline: incident telemetry mapped to a physical/logical dependency graph over time, self-supervised graph-temporal training, counterfactual perturbation or simulation of candidate causes, and ranked root-cause hypotheses linked to remediation actions.

Authors: Fabien Chraim, Jian Zhang, Dominik Janzing, Xiang Song, Christos Faloutsos, John Evans

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Graphical Causal Reasoning for Root Cause Analysis in Cloud Networks
item.description:
Priority: 50/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 42, Paper 40, Empirical 70, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 75

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, citation counts or h-index, DOI or peer-reviewed venue, baseline comparisons, ablations, statistical confidence intervals

Score evidence: Title and abstract explicitly focus on graphical causal reasoning and root cause analysis in cloud networks.; Method uses bivariate Granger causality, conditional independence tests, causal graph traversal, and lag-dependent conditional probabilities.; Evaluation reports 35 labeled production incidents, 85.7% root-cause recall, and 74.3% exact match.; Production use is reported for over 800 real-world incidents with qualitative engineer feedback.

TL;DR: The paper proposes a graph-based causal discovery and inference system for root cause analysis of cloud network incidents, evaluated on labeled production incidents and deployed at scale.

Problem: Cloud network incidents are difficult to diagnose because large-scale cloud networks are complex, dynamic systems where rule-based automation can struggle with dimensionality and causal ambiguity.

Method: The paper introduces a spatiotemporal grouping strategy and automation ontology to reduce problem dimensionality, constructs causal graphs from binary time-series data using bivariate Granger causality plus conditional independence tests, and performs time-aware root cause scoring by traversing the causal graph with edge-specific lag-dependent conditional probabilities.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element, based on the abstract, is an operational RCA framework that combines causal discovery, ontology-based dimensionality reduction, and interpretable time-lag-aware probabilistic inference for cloud network incidents rather than relying on hand-built rules alone.

Main result: On 35 labeled production incidents from a major cloud provider, the system recalled the correct root cause in 85.7% of cases and exactly matched it in 74.3%; it has also been used in over 800 real-world production incidents with positive qualitative feedback.

Limitation: The abstract does not mention baselines, ablations, statistical uncertainty, public code, public data, detailed incident diversity, or robustness analysis, so the strength of the empirical comparison and reproducibility are unclear.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query 'causal discovery' because it applies graph-based causal discovery and causal reasoning directly to a real operational root cause analysis setting.

Tags: causal discovery, root cause analysis, cloud networks, Granger causality, causal graphs, production incidents, network operations

Figure note: A useful figure would show the full RCA pipeline: incident signals converted to binary time series, spatiotemporal grouping and ontology reduction, causal graph construction, lag-aware probabilistic traversal, and final ranked root-cause output.

Authors: Fabien Chraim, Dominik Janzing, John Evans

Query: causal discovery, causal reasoning, causality

PDF

item.title: GeoHAT: Geometry-Adaptive Hybrid Action Transformer for Mobile Manipulation
item.description:
Priority: 53/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 34, Paper 40, Empirical 72, Reproducibility 32, Manuscript 70

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index signals, DOI or venue, code repository, public data or exact benchmark setup, ablation results

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper in cs.RO, published 2026-06-11.; Reports 79.3% mean success rate on ManiSkill-HAB.; Claims +23.7% over strongest baseline.; Claims real-world experiments with consistent improvements, without abstract-level metrics.

TL;DR: GeoHAT is a diffusion-based mobile manipulation framework that selectively injects dense per-pixel 3D geometry into vision-foundation features and decouples arm/base action decoding for whole-body control.

Problem: The paper studies whole-body mobile manipulation, where a robot must coordinate a mobile base and manipulator under changing viewpoints, noisy depth, and different control requirements for arm and base actions.

Method: GeoHAT uses a lightweight Fourier spatial encoder to convert dense per-pixel 3D coordinates into geometric tokens, depth-validity-gated fusion to inject those tokens into vision foundation model features, and a Hybrid Whole-Body Action Decoder that separates arm and base action subspaces with sparse cross-attention plus causal temporal modeling.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is geometry-adaptive fusion: dense 3D information is injected only when depth is reliable and only where useful, avoiding a separate 3D backbone while preserving pretrained visual semantics; action generation is also explicitly decomposed for arm versus base control instead of using one monolithic action vector.

Main result: On ManiSkill-HAB, GeoHAT reports a 79.3% mean success rate, exceeding the strongest baseline by 23.7%; the abstract also claims consistent real-world improvements over all baselines, but gives no real-world metrics.

Limitation: The abstract does not report ablations, code availability, dataset details, statistical variance, real-world task counts, hardware setup, or failure cases, so robustness and reproducibility are unclear.

Relevance: The match to the configured query 'causality' appears to come from the phrase 'causal temporal modeling' for action dependencies, not from causal inference or causal representation learning; relevance is therefore indirect unless the feed includes temporal causal modeling in robotics policies.

Tags: robotics, mobile manipulation, whole-body control, diffusion policy, 3D geometry, vision foundation models, causal temporal modeling

Figure note: A useful image would be the GeoHAT pipeline: RGB-D input, Fourier spatial encoder, gated geometry-token fusion into vision foundation features, and the hybrid arm/base action decoder with sparse cross-attention and causal temporal modeling.

Authors: Xiangyu Zhu, Renjun Wu, Luzhou Ge, Jinyan Liu, Xuesong Li

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: MoVerse: Real-Time Video World Modeling with Panoramic Gaussian Scaffold
item.description:
Priority: 43/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 33, Paper 45, Empirical 45, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 70

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index metadata, DOI, journal, or conference acceptance, citation count or influential-citation signal, public code or repository, public datasets or benchmark names

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-11 in cs.CV.; Method includes topology-aware diffusion, a 3D Gaussian scaffold, and a Gaussian-conditioned video renderer.; The abstract reports 8 FPS on a single NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPU.; The matched query is 'causality', but the abstract only uses 'causal' to describe an autoregressive streaming student model.

TL;DR: MoVerse proposes a real-time single-image video world model that expands a narrow-FOV image into a 360-degree scene, lifts it into a persistent 3D Gaussian scaffold, and renders controllable camera trajectories as photorealistic streaming video.

Problem: The paper studies how to create an interactively navigable, temporally coherent world from a single narrow-field-of-view image, despite missing surrounding context, incomplete geometry, and the need for controllable real-time rendering.

Method: MoVerse separates world construction from observation rendering: topology-aware diffusion first generates a gravity-aligned 360-degree panorama, panoramic geometry-aware residual prediction lifts it into a persistent 3D Gaussian scaffold, and a Gaussian-conditioned video renderer produces video along user-specified camera paths. A bidirectional diffusion teacher is distilled into a causal autoregressive student for bounded-latency streaming.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element claimed in the abstract is the combination of panoramic completion, persistent Gaussian spatial memory, and distilled causal video rendering to enable single-image interactive scene roaming rather than offline novel-view synthesis only.

Main result: The abstract reports real-time scene roaming at 8 FPS on a single NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPU.

Limitation: The abstract does not report datasets, baselines, quantitative visual-quality metrics, user studies, ablations, failure cases, code release, or robustness across scene types, so empirical strength is unclear from the supplied information.

Relevance: The paper matched the configured query 'causality' only through the term 'causal autoregressive student'; it is about causal streaming generation, not causal inference, causal discovery, or counterfactual reasoning. Relevance to a causality-focused feed is therefore weak unless the researcher tracks causal generative video architectures.

Tags: computer-vision, world-modeling, video-generation, 3d-gaussian-splatting, single-image-3d, panorama-generation, real-time-rendering, causal-autoregressive-modeling

Figure note: A useful image would be the MoVerse pipeline figure showing narrow-FOV input, 360-degree panorama completion, 3D Gaussian scaffold construction, and Gaussian-conditioned causal video rendering along user camera trajectories.

Authors: Yang Zhou, Ziheng Wang, Yuqin Lu, Haofeng Liu, Jun Liang, Shengfeng He, Jing Li

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Navigating the Safety-Fidelity Trade-off: Massive-Variate Time Series Forecasting for Power Systems via Probabilistic Scenarios
item.description:
Priority: 45/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 12, Paper 35, Empirical 72, Reproducibility 45, Manuscript 78

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations are not supplied., Author citation or h-index information is not supplied., No DOI, venue, journal, or conference acceptance is supplied., No code repository is mentioned in the abstract or metadata., No explicit public data access statement is supplied., No ablation study is described in the abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-11 in cs.LG.; Benchmark covers six transmission grids with 2,000 to 36,964 jointly forecasted channels.; Targets are generated by AC power-flow solves.; Evaluation includes Safety_mBrier, NECV, CVaR-alpha, CRPS, and Distortion.

TL;DR: PowerPhase introduces a massive-variate probabilistic forecasting benchmark for transmission-grid power systems and PowerForge, a scenario-based quantile forecaster that explicitly targets a safety-fidelity trade-off.

Problem: The paper studies probabilistic forecasting for large multivariate power-system trajectories where channels have different physical meanings and operational constraints, a setting the authors argue is not covered by existing multivariate or power-system benchmarks at scale.

Method: It proposes PowerPhase, a benchmark across six transmission grids with 2,000 to 36,964 jointly forecasted channels generated through AC power-flow solves, plus constraint-aware metrics. It also proposes PowerForge, a scenario-based quantile forecasting model with type-specific decoding heads and a causal bridge between variable groups.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element is the combination of very large-scale probabilistic multivariate forecasting, physics-derived power-flow targets, constraint-aware risk/safety metrics, and an explicit safety-fidelity evaluation trade-off; PowerForge adds a causal-bridge design between variable groups rather than treating all channels as homogeneous.

Main result: From the abstract: across eight baselines and three seeds, distributional accuracy and constraint satisfaction produce different model rankings, and PowerForge achieves the best average rank on every grid.

Limitation: The abstract does not report absolute metric values, ablations, statistical uncertainty beyond three seeds, code availability, public data access details, or evidence that the causal bridge itself is responsible for the performance gain.

Relevance: The paper matched the query 'causality' because PowerForge includes a causal bridge between variable groups, though the abstract frames the main contribution more around probabilistic forecasting, power-system constraints, and benchmark design than causal inference.

Tags: probabilistic forecasting, massive-variate time series, power systems, benchmark, constraint-aware metrics, causal bridge, quantile forecasting

Figure note: A useful image would be a framework figure showing the PowerPhase benchmark pipeline from grid scenarios through AC power-flow solves to probabilistic forecasts and constraint-aware safety metrics, plus the PowerForge causal bridge between variable groups.

Authors: Kaijie Xu, Anqi Wang, Xilin Dai

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: SkillCAT: Contrastive Assessment and Topology-Aware Skill Self-Evolution for LLM Agents
item.description:
TL;DR: SkillCAT is a training-free framework for LLM-agent skill self-evolution that uses contrastive causal evidence from multiple trajectories, patch replay assessment, and topology-aware skill routing to improve reusable skill documents.

Problem: The paper studies how LLM agents can convert execution trajectories into reliable reusable skills, addressing weaknesses in existing pipelines that learn from only one trajectory per task, merge unverified skill patches, and load the entire skill corpus at inference time.

Method: SkillCAT separates skill evolution into three stages: Contrastive Causal Extraction samples multiple trajectories and compares same-task success/failure pairs to find outcome-explaining evidence; Assessment-Augmented Evolution replays candidate patches on cloned source tasks and retains only patches that preserve or improve outcomes before hierarchical merging; Topology-Aware Task Execution compiles skills into a routable sub-skill topology so inference loads only task-relevant capability nodes.

Key contribution: The main novelty is combining contrastive causal extraction, outcome-preserving patch validation, and topology-aware skill retrieval into a training-free skill self-evolution pipeline, rather than relying on single-trajectory learning, unchecked merging, or full-corpus prompting.

Main result: From the abstract, SkillCAT is evaluated on SpreadsheetBench, WikiTableQuestions, DocVQA, plus cross-model and out-of-distribution settings, and improves average score over baselines by up to 40.40%.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify baseline names, absolute scores, statistical significance, compute or trajectory-sampling cost, or whether gains hold beyond the listed document/table/spreadsheet-style agent benchmarks.

Relevance: This is relevant to the `causality` feed because its core extraction stage explicitly compares success and failure trajectories to identify causal evidence explaining task-outcome differences, applying causal-style reasoning to LLM-agent skill evolution.

Tags: LLM agents, skill self-evolution, causal extraction, contrastive learning, agent benchmarks, training-free framework, skill retrieval

Figure note: A useful image would be the SkillCAT pipeline or architecture figure showing the three stages: Contrastive Causal Extraction, Assessment-Augmented Evolution, and Topology-Aware Task Execution.

Authors: Kunfeng Chen, Qihuang Zhong, Juhua Liu, Bo Du

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Semiparametric Bayesian inference for causal mediation in cluster randomized trials
item.description:
TL;DR: The paper proposes a semiparametric Bayesian inference framework for causal mediation analysis in cluster randomized trials with cluster-level mediators and few clusters.

Problem: It studies how to estimate natural direct and indirect causal effects in cluster randomized trials when mediation occurs through a cluster-level mediator and standard large-sample inference gives unreliable variance estimates and confidence intervals with small numbers of clusters.

Method: The approach combines parametric Bayesian models for the outcome and mediator with a similarity-weighted Bayesian bootstrap that uses a distance metric between clusters to quantify uncertainty while borrowing more information from similar clusters.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element described in the abstract is the similarity-weighted Bayesian bootstrap for mediation inference in small-cluster CRTs, intended to avoid restrictive parametric uncertainty assumptions while improving finite-sample coverage.

Main result: Simulation studies reportedly achieve nominal coverage probability across diverse scenarios, and the method is applied to a cluster randomized trial in Kenya.

Limitation: The abstract does not report effect sizes, sample sizes, comparisons against specific competing methods, or details on how sensitive results are to the chosen cluster distance metric and causal assumptions.

Relevance: This is directly relevant to the query "causality" because it develops inference methods for causal mediation, specifically estimating natural direct and indirect effects under causal assumptions in randomized clustered settings.

Tags: causal mediation, cluster randomized trials, Bayesian inference, semiparametric methods, Bayesian bootstrap, finite-sample inference, natural direct effect, natural indirect effect

Figure note: No direct image URL is available from the provided metadata. A useful image would be a framework diagram showing the CRT structure, treatment, cluster-level mediator, outcome model, and similarity-weighted Bayesian bootstrap uncertainty step.

Authors: Woojung Bae, Michael Daniels, Joseph Hogan, Rajesh Vedanthan, Stavroula Chrysanthopoulou

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Mining Architectural Quality Under Agentic AI Adoption: A Causal Study of Java Repositories
item.description:
TL;DR: This paper causally studies whether agentic AI coding tool adoption changes software architectural quality in Java repositories, finding that smell density falls mainly because code size grows, not because smell counts improve.

Problem: The paper addresses the lack of causal evidence on whether AI coding tools, especially agentic 'vibe coding' workflows, affect architecture-level software quality rather than only code-level metrics.

Method: The authors mine 151 open-source Java repositories, identify 74 with detectable agentic AI adoption and 77 propensity-matched controls, build 1,811 monthly Arcan architectural snapshots, and estimate effects using a staggered difference-in-differences design with the Borusyak imputation estimator.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution is applying a recent causal mining design for AI-tool adoption to architecture-level outcomes, while decomposing architectural smell density into raw smell counts and lines of code to show how density metrics can be misleading.

Main result: Total architectural smell counts are essentially unchanged (+1.1%, p = 0.82), lines of code increase substantially (+12.8%, p = 0.003), and architectural smell density declines by 6.7% (p = 0.004), implying a denominator effect rather than architectural improvement.

Limitation: The abstract supports causal identification checks but does not establish whether adoption detection captures all AI use, whether Java open-source repositories generalize to other ecosystems, or whether architectural quality beyond Arcan smell metrics changes.

Relevance: This is directly relevant to the 'causality' feed because it uses a staggered difference-in-differences causal design to estimate the impact of agentic AI adoption on software architecture outcomes.

Tags: causal inference, difference-in-differences, software engineering, AI coding tools, architectural smells, mining software repositories

Figure note: A useful image would be the paper's causal study pipeline or event-study figure showing agentic AI adoption timing, treated versus matched control repositories, and changes in smell counts, LOC, and smell density.

Authors: Oliver Aleksander Larsen, Mahyar T. Moghaddam

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: HYDRA-X: Native Unified Multimodal Models with Holistic Visual Tokenizers
item.description:
TL;DR: HYDRA-X proposes a unified multimodal model with a single ViT-based holistic tokenizer for both images and videos, using causal temporal attention, hierarchical temporal compression, and teacher-supervised decompression to support understanding, generation, and editing.

Problem: The paper studies how unified multimodal models can tokenize diverse visual inputs, especially images and videos, into one compact representation space without separate image/video tokenizers or inefficient full spatiotemporal processing.

Method: HYDRA-X uses a single Vision Transformer tokenizer with frame-level causal temporal attention for reconstruction, hierarchical temporal compression for video efficiency, and a lightweight decompressor trained with joint image-video teacher supervision to inject semantic awareness into the latent space. It also moves source-target interaction for editing into the tokenizer latent space rather than relying on LLM-level semantic interaction.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is a native unified image-video tokenizer inside one ViT, plus evidence that causal temporal attention and hierarchical temporal compression are better design choices than full spatiotemporal attention or single-step compression for this setting.

Main result: The abstract reports strong performance across image and video understanding and generation tasks at a 7B dense model scale, and says latent-level editing improves consistency and convergence, but it does not provide specific benchmark numbers.

Limitation: The abstract does not include quantitative results, dataset details, compute cost, comparison baselines, or evidence about robustness and scalability beyond the stated 7B instantiation.

Relevance: The match to the query 'causality' is narrow but real: the paper uses frame-level causal temporal attention as a core architectural finding for video reconstruction, though it is not about causal inference or causal reasoning.

Tags: multimodal models, visual tokenization, video tokenization, Vision Transformer, causal temporal attention, hierarchical temporal compression, image-video generation, editing

Figure note: A useful image would be the HYDRA-X architecture or tokenizer pipeline figure showing the single ViT tokenizer, causal temporal attention, hierarchical temporal compression, decompressor, and latent-level editing interaction.

Authors: Guozhen Zhang, Xuerui Qiu, Yutao Cui, Tianhui Song, Changlin Li, Junzhe Li, Tao Huang, Xiao Zhang, Yang Li, Jianbing Wu, Miles Yang, Zhao Zhong, Liefeng Bo, Limin Wang

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Causal invariance in graphical models with latent variables
item.description:
TL;DR: The paper studies when causal invariance methods for identifying causal parents remain valid in graphical models where some parent variables are latent.

Problem: Causal invariance can identify causal parents by exploiting stable effects across experimental settings, but unobserved parents can make the observed-variable graph non-DAG-like and non-unique, undermining standard causal discovery assumptions.

Method: The paper gives a graphical and statistical characterization of induced graphs over observed variables under relevant latent-parent configurations, then formalizes conditions under which invariance of causal effects is preserved for observed-parent identification.

Key contribution: It establishes formal necessary and sufficient conditions for testing causal invariance in the presence of latent parents, specifically for a multivariate Gaussian target, rather than assuming all causal parents are observed or that the observed graph remains a DAG.

Main result: The abstract reports theoretical results: characterization of induced graphs and necessary and sufficient invariance-testing conditions for a multivariate Gaussian target. No empirical benchmark or numerical result is described in the abstract.

Limitation: Evidence appears theoretical from the abstract; it does not report experiments, scalability analysis, robustness beyond the stated latent-parent configurations, or results outside the multivariate Gaussian target setting.

Relevance: This is directly relevant to the 'causal discovery' feed because it addresses a core failure mode in causal parent identification: latent variables that break standard DAG-based causal discovery and invariance assumptions.

Tags: causal discovery, causal invariance, latent variables, graphical models, DAGs, Gaussian models, causal inference

Figure note: A useful image would be a framework or graphical-model figure comparing the full DAG with latent parents to the induced observed-variable graph and showing when causal invariance is preserved.

Authors: Marco Borriero, Monia Lupparelli, Giovanni M. Marchetti, Veronica Vinciotti

Query: causal discovery, causal inference, causality

PDF

item.title: Understanding helpfulness and harmless tension in reward models
item.description:
TL;DR: The paper mechanistically studies why reward models trained for both helpfulness and harmlessness can exhibit objective interference, using activation-based neuron identification and targeted ablations to test causal roles of objective-associated neurons.

Problem: Reward models used in RLHF must balance helpfulness and harmlessness, but the internal representations behind these objectives and the reasons they conflict in mixed-objective training are not well understood.

Method: The authors compare reward models trained with helpfulness-only, harmlessness-only, and mixed objectives, then use activation-based methods to identify neurons associated with each objective and targeted ablations to test their functional and causal effects on model behavior.

Key contribution: The novel contribution is a mechanistic account of alignment tension: objective-associated neurons support their own objective while often suppressing the opposing one, and shared helpfulness/harmlessness neurons appear to have disproportionate behavioral influence.

Main result: From the abstract, mixed-objective reward models often underperform single-objective models, suggesting interference; ablations indicate that identified neurons causally support corresponding objectives and can negatively affect the opposing objective.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify datasets, model scales, evaluation metrics, effect sizes, or whether the findings generalize beyond the studied reward models.

Relevance: The paper is relevant to the matched query 'causality' because it uses targeted neuron ablations to move beyond correlation and test causal contributions of internal activations to reward-model objectives.

Tags: RLHF, reward-models, mechanistic-interpretability, alignment, causal-ablation, helpfulness, harmlessness

Figure note: A useful image would be a framework or pipeline diagram showing the three reward-model training settings, activation-based neuron selection, targeted ablation, and measured effects on helpfulness versus harmlessness.

Authors: Eshaan Tanwar, Pepa Atanasova

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: NTS-CoT: Mitigating Hallucinations in LLM-based News Timeline Summarization with Chain-of-Thought Reasoning
item.description:
TL;DR: NTS-CoT proposes a chain-of-thought-based framework to reduce hallucinations and omissions in LLM-based news timeline summarization.

Problem: The paper studies hallucinations in news timeline summarization, specifically unfaithful content in news summaries and omitted information when summarizing date-event developments from rapidly changing news streams.

Method: It proposes NTS-CoT, a three-module framework: Element-CoT extracts essential news elements for faithful summarization, Date Selection combines temporal saliency with event prominence to choose timestamps, and Causal-CoT reasons about causal relationships to reduce omissions in date-event summaries.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution claimed in the abstract is a targeted treatment of hallucination types in LLM-based TLS, including causal chain-of-thought reasoning as an explicit mechanism for improving date-event coverage.

Main result: The abstract reports that NTS-CoT outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on three TLS benchmarks and in human evaluation, improving TLS performance while mitigating hallucinations; exact metrics are not provided in the abstract.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify the models used, benchmark names, metric values, ablation details, or whether chain-of-thought reasoning introduces latency, cost, or interpretability risks.

Relevance: This paper matches the causality query because one core module, Causal-CoT, explicitly infers causal relationships among events to improve timeline summarization and reduce omitted event information.

Tags: timeline summarization, news summarization, LLM hallucination, chain-of-thought, causal reasoning, event causality, cs.CL

Figure note: A useful image would be the NTS-CoT pipeline diagram showing Element-CoT, Date Selection, and Causal-CoT modules and how causal reasoning feeds date-event summarization.

Authors: Feng Lyu, Huiqin Yan, Sijing Duan, Hao Wu, Shuang Gu, Xue Qiao, Weixu Zhang, Haolun Wu

Query: causality

item.title: When Does Routing Become Interpretable? Causal Probes on Block Attention Residuals
item.description:
TL;DR: The paper tests whether making transformer depth routing explicit via Block Attention Residuals is enough for mechanistic interpretability, finding that exposed routing weights still require causal intervention to establish mechanism.

Problem: It studies whether inspectable cross-layer routing tensors in Block Attention Residual architectures can be interpreted as actual information-flow mechanisms, rather than merely descriptive attention-like summaries.

Method: The author compares two same-scale 0.6B Qwen3-based checkpoints under identical routing-ablation interventions: a vanilla Qwen3 wrapped with a deterministic recency-bias routing schedule, and a Block AttnRes Qwen3 trained from scratch with routing included in optimization.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element is a causal probe of explicit depth-routing weights, showing that architectural exposure of routing creates an interpretable target but does not by itself make routing weights mechanistically meaningful.

Main result: The wrapped baseline shows content-independent routing matching the analytic schedule, while the trained AttnRes model develops three localized routing motifs; however, high average routing mass often fails to match causal importance, and some routed source families carry mass without detectable causal effect.

Limitation: The abstract reports results only for two 0.6B checkpoints and routing-ablation interventions; it does not establish generality across model scales, tasks, architectures, or broader causal-probing methods.

Relevance: This is relevant to the query 'causality' because the central claim depends on causal interventions distinguishing descriptive routing mass from causal contribution in neural network mechanisms.

Tags: causality, mechanistic-interpretability, transformers, routing, residual-connections, ablation, language-models

Figure note: A useful image would be a framework or pipeline figure showing Block Attention Residual routing over earlier depth-source representations, alongside the causal routing-ablation setup and the three discovered routing motifs.

Authors: Aydin Javadov

Query: causality

item.title: MiniPIC: Flexible Position-Independent Caching in <100LOC
item.description:
TL;DR: MiniPIC is a lightweight vLLM modification for reusing KV cache entries for recurring prompt spans even when they do not appear at identical prefix positions.

Problem: The paper studies inefficient prefill in retrieval-augmented and agentic LLM workloads, where repeated structured spans such as documents or code files cannot be reused by standard prefix caching unless they share identical prefixes.

Method: It proposes Minimalistic PIC, a vLLM design that stores unrotated K vectors, applies RoPE inside attention using per-request logical positions, and exposes token-level cache-reuse primitives: block-aligned padding, span separator, and prompt depend.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is that multiple position-independent caching schemes, including Block-Attention, EPIC, and Prompt Cache, can be expressed inside one running vLLM instance with fewer than 100 lines of core-engine changes plus a custom attention backend, while integrating with KV cache CPU offload.

Main result: From the abstract, on 2WikiMultihopQA, MiniPIC with interleaved scheduling improves prefill throughput by 49% over baseline vLLM, reduces cached-span time-to-first-token by up to two orders of magnitude, keeps linear prefill scaling for uncached spans, and has 5.7% worst-case overhead.

Limitation: The abstract reports results on one named benchmark and does not provide evidence about broader model families, workload diversity, correctness impacts beyond caching behavior, or implementation complexity outside vLLM with the custom attention backend.

Relevance: The paper matched the query 'causality' likely because it changes effective block-level causal attention structure; it is relevant to causal masking/attention mechanics in LLM inference systems, but it does not appear to study causal inference or causality as a scientific modeling topic.

Tags: LLM inference, KV cache, vLLM, position-independent caching, RoPE, causal attention, retrieval-augmented generation, agentic workloads

Figure note: A useful image would be the MiniPIC pipeline or attention/cache architecture figure showing unrotated KV storage, per-request RoPE application, span separators, prompt dependencies, and block-level causal attention changes.

Authors: Nathan Ordonez, Thomas Parnell

Query: causality

item.title: MP3: Multi-Period Pattern Pre-training forSpatio-Temporal Forecasting
item.description:
TL;DR: MP3 is a plug-and-play pre-training module for spatio-temporal graph neural networks that learns long-horizon multi-period temporal, spatial, and cross-period causal patterns to reduce temporal mirage errors in forecasting.

Problem: The paper studies spatio-temporal forecasting under 'temporal mirage' conditions, where similar short input windows can lead to divergent futures and dissimilar windows can converge, making standard STGNNs unreliable when they rely on incomplete short-window observations.

Method: It proposes Multi-Period Pattern Pre-training (MP3), a pre-training plugin for existing STGNN backbones. MP3 learns multi-period temporal patterns with edge convolution, captures heterogeneous global spatial correlations using a bottleneck projection and global memory bank, and models dependencies across periods with a causality-enhanced Transformer.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution is framing temporal mirage as a multi-period observation and cross-period causality problem, then addressing it with a backbone-agnostic pre-training plugin rather than a replacement forecasting architecture.

Main result: Across five STGNN baselines and five real-world datasets, including a large-scale CA dataset, MP3 reportedly gives consistent improvements, reducing MAE by 4.7% and RMSE by 5.0% on average.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify the exact datasets, evaluation protocols, ablations, computational overhead, or whether the causality-enhanced Transformer identifies causal structure beyond improving predictive performance.

Relevance: The paper matches the 'causality' query because cross-period superposition causality is central to its diagnosis of temporal mirages, and the proposed framework explicitly includes a causality-enhanced Transformer for cross-period dependency modeling.

Tags: spatio-temporal forecasting, STGNN, pre-training, multi-period patterns, causality, time series, graph neural networks

Figure note: A useful image would show the MP3 pipeline: long time-series inputs split into multi-period patterns, temporal edge-convolution modeling, global spatial memory-bank modeling, causality-enhanced cross-period Transformer interaction, and integration into an STGNN backbone.

Authors: Lilan Peng, Yandi Liu, Qingren Yao, Chongshou Li, Tianrui Li

Query: causality

item.title: Demystifying Hidden-State Recurrence: Switchable Latent Reasoning with On-Policy Reinforcement Learning
item.description:
TL;DR: SWITCH makes latent chain-of-thought reasoning trainable with on-policy RL and more causally interpretable by using explicit discrete tokens to enter and exit hidden-state recurrence.

Problem: The paper studies how to make latent chain-of-thought methods based on hidden-state recurrence easier to optimize with standard on-policy reinforcement learning and easier to analyze mechanistically or causally.

Method: It proposes SWITCH, where a model emits <swi> to enter latent reasoning mode and </swi> to exit, trains with a visible-to-latent curriculum, and uses a Switch-GRPO objective that propagates gradients through recurrent latent computation.

Key contribution: The novel idea is to use ordinary discrete boundary tokens as both RL-compatible decision anchors and intervention/probing points for mechanistic analysis of latent reasoning steps.

Main result: According to the abstract, SWITCH consistently outperforms prior hidden-state-recurrence latent reasoning approaches at similar scale, and boundary-token analysis suggests the entry transition performs problem-specific, causally important computation.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify benchmark names, quantitative gains, model scale details, or whether the mechanistic findings generalize beyond the tested tasks and training setup.

Relevance: This is relevant to the query 'causality' because the paper explicitly studies causal intervention and mechanistic analysis of latent reasoning, including identifying causally important hidden-state transitions.

Tags: latent reasoning, chain-of-thought, reinforcement learning, GRPO, mechanistic interpretability, causal intervention, language models

Figure note: A useful image would be the SWITCH pipeline or architecture diagram showing visible reasoning, <swi>/</swi> boundary tokens, recurrent latent steps, GRPO training, and causal probes at the boundary transitions.

Authors: Jiayu Yang, Chao Chen, Shengen Wu, Yinhong Liu, Yuxuan Fan, Lujundong Li, Songning Lai, Chengwei Qin, Zhijiang Guo

item.title: CausalMoE: A Billion-Scale Multimodal Foundation Model for Granger Causal Discovery with Pattern-Routed Heterogeneous Experts
item.description:
TL;DR: CausalMoE proposes a billion-scale multimodal Mixture-of-Experts foundation model for Granger causal discovery that routes time-series patches by latent temporal patterns to improve causal graph recovery under regime shifts.

Problem: The paper studies Granger causal discovery for real-world time series where distribution shifts, dynamic regimes, and heterogeneous temporal patterns cause standard neural methods to learn entangled representations and spurious causal graphs.

Method: It proposes CausalMoE, combining pattern-routed heterogeneous experts for patch-level temporal heterogeneity, causality-aware self-attention across variables for sparse graph recovery via proximal optimization, and multimodal alignment with LLM/VLM priors to regularize causal estimation.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is a billion-scale multimodal Granger causal foundation model that explicitly routes temporal patches to specialized experts and integrates textual and visual priors, rather than using a single shared neural causal discovery model for all regimes.

Main result: The abstract reports new state-of-the-art results on fully supervised benchmarks and improved generalization in few-shot settings where traditional methods fail, but it does not provide quantitative metrics.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify benchmark names, effect sizes, ablation evidence, computational cost, or whether multimodal priors improve causal validity beyond benchmark performance.

Relevance: This directly matches the configured query 'causal discovery' because it targets Granger causal graph recovery and proposes a new neural framework for causality in temporal systems.

Tags: causal discovery, Granger causality, time series, mixture of experts, foundation model, multimodal learning, few-shot generalization

Figure note: A useful image would be the model pipeline showing patch-level pattern routing, heterogeneous experts, causality-aware variable attention, and LLM/VLM prior alignment for causal graph recovery.

Authors: Bo Liu, Di Dai, Jingwei Liu, Jiarui Jin, Xiaocheng Fang, Guangkun Nie, Hongyan Li, Shenda Hong

Query: causal discovery, causality

item.title: SciR: A Controllable Benchmark for Scientific Reasoning in LLMs
item.description:
Priority: 54/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 45, Paper 50, Empirical 65, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 78

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations not supplied., Citation counts or h-index signals not supplied., DOI, venue, or peer-review status not supplied., Code repository availability not stated., Public data or benchmark release details not stated., Exact metrics, benchmark size, model list, and statistical robustness not reported in abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-11 in cs.AI.; Benchmark covers deduction, induction, and causal abduction.; Tasks are generated from formal objects with verifiable answers.; Difficulty is varied along extraction and inference axes.

TL;DR: SciR is a controllable benchmark for evaluating LLM scientific reasoning across deduction, induction, and causal abduction, with separate difficulty controls for information extraction and formal inference.

Problem: The paper studies how to reliably evaluate LLMs on scientific reasoning when human-annotated scientific benchmarks are expensive and lack mechanistic ground truth, while synthetic logic benchmarks often fail to resemble real scientific documents.

Method: SciR generates tasks from formal objects, including deduction trees, inductive rule hypotheses, and causal graphs, then renders them into multi-document scientific discourse using domain-tuned genres. This gives verifiable answers and lets the benchmark independently vary extraction difficulty and inference difficulty.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is a multi-paradigm scientific-reasoning benchmark with parametric control over both document-level extraction difficulty and underlying inference difficulty, including causal abduction as one of the reasoning paradigms.

Main result: The abstract reports experiments on six models: both extraction and inference difficulty hurt all models, the effects compound, scientific rendering hurts even neurosymbolic solver pipelines, and reasoning models such as deepseek-r1 outperform non-reasoning instruct models mainly on the inference axis.

Limitation: The abstract does not report exact datasets, metrics, model list, code/data availability, statistical robustness, or detailed benchmark size, so empirical strength and reproducibility cannot be fully assessed from the supplied metadata.

Relevance: This matches the configured query 'causality' because one SciR track is explicitly based on causal graphs and causal abduction, making it relevant to evaluating causal reasoning in LLMs within scientific-text settings.

Tags: LLM evaluation, scientific reasoning, causal abduction, causal graphs, benchmark, deduction, induction, multi-document reasoning

Figure note: A useful image would show the SciR pipeline: formal objects for deduction, induction, and causal graphs feeding controllable scientific document rendering, then evaluation across extraction and inference difficulty axes.

item.title: Circuit Synchronization Precedes Generalization: Causal Evidence from Fourier Structure in Grokking Transformers
item.description:
Priority: 54/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 38, Paper 45, Empirical 78, Reproducibility 45, Manuscript 70

Missing quality signals: No affiliation or institution metadata supplied., No citation count or h-index metadata supplied., No DOI, venue, journal, or conference signal supplied., No code repository or reproducibility artifact mentioned., No public dataset artifact mentioned., No explicit limitations section visible from the abstract.

Score evidence: Introduces Frequency Synchronization Degree as a normalized, permutation-tested metric requiring no prior circuit knowledge.; Reports nine modular-addition configurations over primes p in {53, 71, 97, 113, 131} and three seeds.; FSD leads grokking in all nine cases with mean lead +1722 steps and sign-test p approximately 0.004.; Forking at the FSD-ceiling step and varying weight decay is reported to produce monotone earlier grokking with Delta_t proportional to 1/lambda.

TL;DR: The paper proposes Frequency Synchronization Degree as an early, circuit-agnostic predictor of grokking in modular-addition transformers and reports causal intervention evidence linking the delay between circuit synchronization and generalization to weight decay.

Problem: It studies why transformer grokking on modular arithmetic occurs suddenly, with emphasis on the timing, causal structure, and controllability of the Fourier circuit thought to support generalization.

Method: The paper introduces Frequency Synchronization Degree, a normalized permutation-tested metric for Fourier circuit synchronization that requires no prior circuit knowledge, then evaluates it across modular-addition runs, compares it with a restricted-logit loss baseline, forks training at the FSD-ceiling step while varying weight decay, and performs architecture ablations.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is that Fourier-circuit synchronization can be detected before grokking without hand-identifying the circuit, and that the remaining delay to grokking follows a weight-decay-controlled law, providing causal rather than purely correlational evidence.

Main result: Across nine modular-addition configurations, FSD synchronizes 500-3000 steps before grokking with mean lead of 1722 steps and positive lead in all nine cases; forked-training interventions reportedly show earlier grokking monotonically with larger weight decay, with Delta_t approximately proportional to 1/lambda.

Limitation: Evidence is limited to modular addition grokking settings from the abstract; no code, public artifacts, affiliations, citation signals, robustness beyond the stated primes/seeds, or broader task transfer are provided in the supplied metadata.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the causality query because it claims direct causal evidence about how regularization controls the timing between Fourier-circuit synchronization and generalization in grokking transformers.

Tags: grokking, mechanistic-interpretability, causality, Fourier-circuits, transformers, regularization, modular-arithmetic

Figure note: A useful image would show the proposed FSD pipeline and a timeline overlay where FSD synchronization, restricted-logit loss, and grokking occur across training steps.

Authors: Achyuthan Sivasankar

item.title: Order Is Not Control
item.description:
Priority: 44/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 30, Paper 45, Empirical 55, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 70

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index metadata, DOI, journal, or conference status, citation or influential-citation counts, open-source code, public data or benchmark identifiers

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-11 in cs.LG, cs.AI, and cs.CL.; Abstract reports LLM component-sign accuracy of 72.8-73.7%, rising to 84.3-84.8% on nonzero components.; Abstract reports held-out observer prediction accuracies of 93.6% and 91.7%.; Abstract explicitly states several limits: no deployable pre-generation control, no hidden/logit causal sufficiency, no biological-to-LLM coordinate identity, and no literal thermodynamic quantities.

TL;DR: The paper argues that order-inducing interventions are not sufficient for control and proposes a receiver-gated response-operator framework for identifying local admitted control across biological systems, LLMs, adapters, and stochastic-operator settings.

Problem: It studies how to distinguish mere order, alignment, steering, interpretability structure, or perturbation response from actual control in systems where interventions can have medium-, bath-, receiver-, and comparator-dependent effects.

Method: The paper proposes a denominator-indexed, receiver-gated response law: an operator mapping material state, action or drive, bath, and receiver state to response displacement, sinks, effort, and basin projection. It evaluates this framing across biological panels, LLM generated-output panels, constitution-conditioned adapters, and stochastic-operator panels.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is a control criterion based on local receiver-gated response laws rather than coordinate identity, order induction, or controller attribution: control is assigned only when finite effort moves a target or outcome-readout class under the same denominator while damage, null/evasive behavior, invalid format, overdrive, and unnecessary effort remain bounded.

Main result: From the abstract, LLM response vectors are predictable at 72.8-73.7% component-sign accuracy, increasing to 84.3-84.8% on nonzero components; held-out observers predict system-effect and target/oracle families at 93.6% and 91.7% accuracy. Biological panels are described as supporting physical response-operator evidence, but detailed sample sizes, datasets, and controls are not provided in the abstract.

Limitation: The abstract explicitly leaves deployable pre-generation control, hidden/logit causal sufficiency, biological-to-LLM coordinate identity, and literal thermodynamic quantities outside scope. It also does not provide code, data, baselines, ablations, statistical robustness, sample sizes, or venue/peer-review signals in the supplied metadata.

Relevance: The paper matched the query 'causality' because it proposes criteria for causal control rather than correlation/order: interventions are evaluated by receiver-gated response laws, finite-effort target movement, bounded failure modes, and separations between measured opportunity and deployable action policies.

Tags: causality, control, AI alignment, interpretability, LLM steering, response operators, stochastic operators, biological systems

Figure note: A useful image would be a framework diagram showing the denominator-indexed response operator: material state, action/drive, bath, and receiver state flowing into response displacement, sinks, effort, and basin projection, with bounded failure modes used to decide local admitted control.

Authors: Gareth Seneque, Lap-Hang Ho, Nafise Erfanian Saeedi, Jeffrey Molendijk, Tim Elson

item.title: MAStrike: Shapley-Guided Collusive Red-Teaming on Multi-Agent Systems
item.description:
Priority: 54/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 40, Paper 45, Empirical 72, Reproducibility 45, Manuscript 75

Missing quality signals: Affiliations not supplied., Citation count and h-index not supplied., No DOI, venue, or peer-review status supplied., No code repository or dataset URL supplied., No numerical metrics or effect sizes in the abstract., No ablation, robustness, or statistical significance details in the abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-11 in cs.CR and cs.AI.; Abstract claims a closed-loop framework for collusive red-teaming in hierarchical MAS.; Abstract claims first agent-level Shapley value analysis for MAS robustness.; Abstract claims structured causal diagnosis is used to refine attacks.

TL;DR: MAStrike proposes a Shapley-guided closed-loop framework for finding collusive adversarial coalitions in hierarchical multi-agent systems and red-teaming them with coordinated role-aware manipulations.

Problem: The paper studies safety and security failures in hierarchical multi-agent systems where attacks can be distributed across role-specialized agents through privilege escalation, collusion, and coordinated bypasses of system defenses.

Method: It proposes MAStrike, a closed-loop collusive red-teaming framework that estimates agent-level Shapley values for robustness, uses those attributions to identify vulnerable coalitions, generates coordinated role-aware adversarial manipulations, and iteratively refines attacks using structured causal diagnosis of failed attempts.

Key contribution: The main novelty claimed is agent-level Shapley value analysis for MAS robustness combined with coalition-guided, causal-diagnosis-driven adversarial attack generation, rather than heuristic target selection or isolated message perturbations.

Main result: The abstract reports extensive experiments on hierarchical MAS across finance, software engineering, and CRM, using multiple frontier models, where MAStrike substantially outperforms heuristic baselines; no quantitative effect sizes or metric values are provided in the abstract.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide numerical results, benchmark details, code or data availability, exact baselines, ablations, statistical robustness, or evidence about scalability of Shapley estimation.

Relevance: The paper matched the query 'causality' because MAStrike uses structured causal diagnosis to attribute attack failures to uncompromised agents and refine collusive red-team strategies in multi-agent systems.

Tags: multi-agent systems, red-teaming, AI security, Shapley values, causal diagnosis, collusion, hierarchical agents

Figure note: A useful image would show the MAStrike pipeline: Shapley-based agent attribution, vulnerable coalition selection, coordinated adversarial manipulation, causal diagnosis of blocked attacks, and iterative refinement across a hierarchical MAS topology.

item.title: Where Computation Lives Inside TabPFN: Causal Localisation of Attention Head Function
item.description:
Priority: 43/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 30, Paper 35, Empirical 60, Reproducibility 30, Manuscript 70

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations are not supplied., Citation counts and h-index signals are not supplied., No DOI, conference, journal, or peer-review signal is supplied., No code repository or implementation artifact is mentioned in the supplied metadata or abstract., No public dataset or benchmark suite is mentioned beyond synthetic regression datasets., No baselines, statistical significance, robustness checks, or full metric details are visible in the abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-06-11 in cs.LG.; Abstract claims first causal mechanistic analysis of TabPFN 2.5.; Methods named in abstract: activation patching, ablation, attention entropy, and contrastive activation steering.; Empirical setting visible in abstract: two synthetic regression datasets.

TL;DR: The paper applies causal mechanistic interpretability tools to TabPFN 2.5 and reports that feature-wise attention computation is temporally localized, with one dominant head carrying substantially more causal importance than the others on synthetic regression tasks.

Problem: The paper studies where and how computation is distributed across feature-wise attention heads and layers inside the tabular foundation model TabPFN 2.5, with an emphasis on causal localization rather than purely correlational attention inspection.

Method: It uses activation patching, head ablation, and attention-entropy analysis on two synthetic regression datasets, and additionally tests inference-time contrastive activation steering.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is a causal mechanistic analysis of a tabular foundation model, identifying task-dependent temporal specialization of attention-head function and contrasting TabPFN's context-dependent computation with steering behavior commonly studied in language models.

Main result: From the abstract: one attention head is causally dominant by about 2x to 5x at its peak layer, the dominant layer shifts with task complexity, and attention entropy aligns with patching evidence for the active layers. Contrastive activation steering does not transfer across samples.

Limitation: Evidence is limited in the supplied abstract to two synthetic regression datasets; there is no visible claim about real-world tabular benchmarks, statistical robustness, open-source code, public data, broader ablations, or external validation.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the configured query 'causality' because it uses causal mechanistic methods, including activation patching and ablation, to localize functional components inside a foundation model.

Tags: causal-mechanistic-interpretability, tabular-foundation-models, TabPFN, activation-patching, attention-heads, ablation, in-context-learning, causality

item.description:

Score evidence: Abstract states the setting includes labeled outcomes/regressors plus unlabeled auxiliary regressors.; Abstract derives an efficient influence function and efficiency bound for PPCI.; Abstract proposes EE-DML-PPCI and TMLE-DML-PPCI estimators.; Abstract claims both estimators match the derived efficiency bound.

TL;DR: The paper proposes a semi-supervised, efficiency-theoretic framework for causal and structural parameter estimation that uses unlabeled auxiliary regressors to reduce asymptotic variance relative to labeled-data-only estimators.

Problem: The study addresses semiparametric efficient estimation of causal and structural parameters when labeled observations include outcomes and regressors, while additional unlabeled auxiliary regressors are also available.

Method: It introduces prediction-powered causal inference (PPCI), derives the efficient influence function and efficiency bound, and combines that influence-function construction with debiased machine learning to define EE-DML-PPCI and TMLE-DML-PPCI estimators. It also develops semi-supervised generalized Riesz regression for estimating the Riesz representer with convergence guarantees.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is that unlabeled auxiliary regressors can be incorporated into causal/structural parameter estimation to attain a smaller asymptotic variance than the labeled-data-only efficiency bound, with DML/TMLE-style estimators matching the derived semi-supervised efficiency bound.

Main result: From the abstract, the main result is theoretical: both proposed estimators are claimed to have asymptotic variances matching the newly derived efficiency bound. No finite-sample empirical result is reported in the abstract.

Limitation: The abstract does not report simulations, real-data experiments, baselines, ablations, robustness checks, code, data, DOI, venue, or affiliation/citation metadata, so the practical magnitude of the efficiency gain and reproducibility are unclear from the supplied information.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the configured query "causal inference" and is especially relevant to researchers interested in semiparametric efficiency, debiased machine learning, TMLE, Riesz representers, and semi-supervised causal estimation.

item.title: ShrinkageTrees: An R Package for Bayesian Tree Ensembles for Survival Analysis and Causal Inference
item.description:
TL;DR: ShrinkageTrees introduces an R package implementing regularized Bayesian additive regression tree ensembles for censored survival outcomes and causal effect estimation.

Problem: The paper addresses flexible survival modeling and causal inference when outcomes are right- or interval-censored, especially in high-dimensional covariate settings where standard Bayesian tree ensembles may overfit or lack suitable regularization.

Method: It implements Bayesian additive regression tree models in an accelerated failure time framework, with optional decomposition into prognostic and treatment-effect components, using depth-penalizing and Dirichlet splitting priors for tree structure plus global-local shrinkage priors for step heights.

Key contribution: The abstract claims the first implementation of the Horseshoe Forest, which applies a horseshoe prior to tree step heights, and packages this with survival and causal inference workflows in R.

Main result: No empirical benchmark or numerical result is reported in the abstract; the stated result is software capability, including fitting, prediction, causal effect estimation, convergence diagnostics, Rcpp acceleration, and multi-chain MCMC.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide evidence on accuracy, calibration, runtime, scalability, or comparisons against existing survival or causal inference methods.

Relevance: This is directly relevant to the matched query 'causal inference' because it provides Bayesian tree ensemble tools for estimating treatment effects, particularly with censored survival outcomes and high-dimensional covariates.

Tags: causal inference, survival analysis, Bayesian additive regression trees, R package, censored outcomes, Horseshoe Forest, MCMC

Figure note: A useful image would be a workflow or architecture diagram showing censored survival data entering the AFT Bayesian tree ensemble, the prognostic/treatment-effect decomposition, regularization priors, and outputs for prediction and causal effect estimation.

item.title: The Impossibility of Eliciting Latent Knowledge
item.description:
TL;DR: The paper formalizes eliciting latent knowledge in AI systems using Causal Influence Diagrams and proves that behavior-only feedback cannot guarantee an honest agent, even with perfect training feedback.

Problem: It studies how to train advanced AI systems to honestly report their beliefs about latent environmental variables that humans cannot directly observe, a problem known as eliciting latent knowledge (ELK).

Method: The authors use Causal Influence Diagrams to model the relationship between an agent's training environment, its subjective world representation, observable variables, latent variables, honesty, and goal misgeneralisation.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution is a formal CID-based treatment of ELK that distinguishes honest reporting from merely producing answers humans would judge as true, plus an impossibility theorem for behavior-only feedback strategies.

Main result: The abstract states an impossibility theorem: no feedback-based training strategy that depends only on agent behavior can guarantee production of an honest agent with certainty, even when training feedback is perfect.

Limitation: The abstract does not report empirical experiments, benchmarks, or practical mitigation evidence; the result appears theoretical and applies under the paper's stated formal assumptions.

Relevance: This is relevant to the query 'causality' because the paper uses Causal Influence Diagrams as the core formal framework for reasoning about hidden variables, agent beliefs, feedback, and causal structure in AI alignment.

Tags: causality, causal influence diagrams, AI alignment, eliciting latent knowledge, honesty, goal misgeneralisation, theoretical AI safety

Figure note: A useful image would be the paper's CID framework diagram showing observable variables, latent variables, agent beliefs, feedback, and behavior in the training environment.

Authors: Korbinian Friedl, Francis Rhys Ward, Paul Yushin Rapoport, Tom Everitt, Jonathan Richens

item.title: Making Foresight Actionable: Repurposing Representation Alignment in World Action Models
item.description:
TL;DR: The paper studies why visually plausible World Action Model rollouts can still yield poor robot actions and proposes AGRA, a representation-alignment objective that makes video diffusion features more useful for action decoding.

Problem: World Action Models for robot manipulation use video generation to predict future scene evolution before choosing actions, but the abstract reports that plausible generated futures do not reliably produce accurate low-level control actions.

Method: The authors diagnose the issue with action-head attention analysis and causal interventions, then introduce AGRA: an Action-Grounded Representation Alignment objective that aligns intermediate video diffusion features with spatially coherent semantic representations from a foundation visual encoder.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution is reframing the world-model-to-action interface as a representation mismatch problem: hidden states optimized for visual reconstruction are regularized to become spatially and semantically organized for action control, rather than assuming better video prediction directly implies better actions.

Main result: On real-world manipulation tasks, AGRA reportedly improves attention to task-relevant interaction regions, object localization, affordance understanding, robustness to task-irrelevant perturbations, in-distribution performance, and out-of-distribution generalization over a baseline World Action Model.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide quantitative results, task suite details, baseline names beyond a baseline WAM, or evidence about computational cost and scalability across robot platforms.

Relevance: This is relevant to the `causality` feed because the paper explicitly uses causal interventions to identify why action decoders fail under task-irrelevant perturbations, though the main contribution is representation alignment for robot world-action models rather than a general causal inference method.

Tags: robot manipulation, world action models, video diffusion, representation alignment, causal interventions, attention analysis, affordance learning, out-of-distribution generalization

Figure note: A useful image would show the AGRA pipeline: video diffusion world-model features feeding an action decoder, the semantic foundation-encoder alignment objective, and attention or causal-intervention maps highlighting task-relevant versus task-irrelevant regions.

Authors: Lu Qiu, Yizhuo Li, Yi Chen, Yuying Ge, Yixiao Ge, Xihui Liu
